text.title: Strangler Fig Migration Pattern Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that distinguish a true strangler-fig migration from a long-running upgrade branch that pretends to be one. Each rule is verifiable from the repo state (router config, PR history, legacy-frozen marker).

rule.statement: Before ANY route is migrated, the migration MUST land an outermost router / boundary that can dispatch to either legacy or new implementations per-route. This boundary MUST be merged to main and operate in production before move-1 begins. Migrations that start with "let's just port one route" before the boundary exists are FORBIDDEN.

rationale.source: Fowler StranglerFigApplication; Next.js / Rails migration retros

rule.rationale: Without a dispatch boundary, every per-route move becomes a hand-coded conditional inside the legacy app, which is itself a migration debt. A real boundary (nginx, middleware, URLconf split, Rack stack) lets each subsequent move be a small config change.

rule.statement: New implementations MUST live BESIDE legacy ones in the same repo, in a clearly separated directory (e.g. `app/` vs `pages/`, `apps/new-frontend/` vs `apps/legacy/`, `engines/admin_v2/` vs `app/controllers/admin/`). A long-running parallel branch is FORBIDDEN. Main MUST remain deployable on every commit.
rule.rationale: Long-running upgrade branches accumulate merge conflicts, drift from main, and become a singular bet that dominates planning. Coexistence in main converts the migration into 100 small bets instead of one big one.

rule.statement: Cutover from legacy to new MUST happen one route (or one module / one bounded surface) at a time, gated by the boundary's per-route config. Each cutover MUST be revertible by flipping a flag or config line. "Switch everything when we're ready" cutovers are FORBIDDEN.
rule.rationale: Big-bang cutovers concentrate risk in a single moment. Per-route cutovers spread risk across N small moments, each with a near-instant rollback. This is the single largest determinant of whether a major migration ships intact.

rule.statement: Once a route is cut over to the new implementation, the legacy code for that route MUST be frozen: no new features, no non-security fixes. A CODEOWNERS rule or a `LEGACY_FROZEN.md` marker MUST encode the freeze. Bug fixes in frozen legacy code are an explicit exception requiring sign-off.
rule.rationale: If legacy can still accept features, the migration never finishes — every developer chooses the path of least resistance, which is the framework they already know. Freezing legacy aligns incentives: shipping new work now requires the new stack.

rule.statement: The migration is COMPLETE only when the legacy code is DELETED from the repo (not just unreachable). A migration that ends with both stacks coexisting "for now" is incomplete and counts against the team's tech-debt budget. A final task in the migration plan MUST be "delete legacy directory + remove boundary + remove dependency".
rule.rationale: Coexistence is the strangler's mid-state, not its end-state. Stopping at coexistence doubles the maintenance surface forever. Forcing deletion as a planned step closes the loop and recovers the velocity the migration was meant to unlock.

rule.statement: Skip Strangler Fig Migration Pattern when any Skip-If condition holds. Do NOT produce an artefact; surface the gap to the requester.
rule.rationale: A methodology applied outside its scope produces low-signal artefacts that pollute the artefact store; the decision tree needs a clean terminator.

example.title: Next.js pages → app router strangler
example.code:
boundary: next.config: `experimental.appDir = true` directory: `pages/` and `app/` coexist; Next dispatches per route automatically.
cutover order (size-ascending): 1. /about (static) → app/about/page.tsx 2. /pricing (static, MDX) → app/pricing/page.tsx 3. /login (form, no SSR auth) → app/login/page.tsx ... up to ... N. /dashboard (auth + SSR) → app/(authenticated)/dashboard/page.tsx
per-route revert: rename app/route/page.tsx → app/route/_disabled.page.tsx → Next falls back to pages/route.tsx automatically.
freeze: pages/*.tsx that already moved get a top-comment marker and CODEOWNERS rule. delete: final PR removes pages/, next.config experimental flag, and any compat shim.
example.note: r1-r5 all satisfied. Each cutover is a 5-50-line PR; main is releasable on every commit.

example.title: The big-bang branch in disguise
example.code:
branch: feat/next-15-app-router (created 4 months ago) status: 87 files changed, 280 commits, 14 conflicts with main plan: "merge when everything is migrated"
reality: - main keeps shipping features the branch must continually rebase against - one bug in the branch blocks the whole team - the only person who knows the branch state quits / goes on holiday - branch is abandoned; team starts over with strangler approach
example.note: r1, r2 violated. A long-running branch IS the failure mode the strangler exists to prevent. Even if delivered, every conflict resolution is a per-PR cost the per-route approach skips.
